organization: Ohio University — Dr. Shawn Ostermann & Ilya Kogan
description:
## Overview
Leading the integration and configuration of Backstage — an open-source internal developer portal — into the university's on-premises Kubernetes platform, with a focus on scalability, reliability, and developer workflow improvements.

## Authentication & User Provisioning
- Implemented **Azure AD SSO** using the Backstage auth plugin, including Microsoft Graph API integration for automated user provisioning and secure authentication flows
- Upcoming work includes enabling **GitHub-based student login** to expand platform access beyond university SSO

## Plugin Integration & Configuration
Configured and integrated a range of standard and community Backstage plugins, each requiring significant setup work including service account creation, secret management, and application wiring:
- **Catalog** — configured GitHub org imports for automated service discovery across university repositories
- **Scaffolder** — authored custom templates for standardized project and service onboarding
- **TechDocs** — enabled in-portal technical documentation rendering
- **Jira** (community plugin) — integrated for issue tracking and project visibility within the portal
- **Prometheus & Grafana** (community plugins) — surfaced real-time Kubernetes metrics and dashboards within the portal

## Infrastructure & Secrets Management
- Managed Kubernetes secrets using **Sealed Secrets**, encrypting secrets at rest and safely storing them in version control
- Configured service accounts and wired credentials into the Backstage pod through Kubernetes secret injection
- Worked across the full integration lifecycle: configuring external service accounts, sealing and deploying secrets, and connecting APIs to the running application

## Environment
Working in a Linux-based environment with on-premises Kubernetes clusters, cloud APIs, and infrastructure automation tooling.

role: Instructor of Record — CS1400: Fundamentals of Computing
description:
Sole instructor for an introductory computing course of ~25 students, responsible for the full instructional lifecycle from curriculum design to final grading.

- Designed and delivered lectures, quizzes, programming assignments, exams, and supplemental materials
- Taught foundational concepts including computational thinking, problem solving, algorithms, **Python** programming, and contemporary issues in computer science
- Provided timely, constructive feedback to support student growth and learning outcomes

## Overview
Conducted NASA-funded systems and networking research focused on evaluating the performance and behavior of **ION** (Interplanetary Overlay Network) — a delay-tolerant networking (DTN) implementation — across diverse system environments.

## Research Focus
DTN protocols like ION are designed to handle the extreme latency, disruption, and packet loss of challenged network links. The research investigated how ION behaved under varied system conditions to characterize its performance and reliability.

## QEMU Virtualization
A key challenge was testing ION across different operating systems and hardware architectures without access to physical legacy machines. Built and configured **QEMU** virtual machine environments emulating non-x86 architectures including **SPARC-32** (Solaris) and **ARM** (Raspbian), set up SSH jump host and bridge networking to make VMs remotely accessible, and documented the full environment setup for reproducibility. Once stable, the VMs were registered as worker nodes in **Buildbot** — a CI-style test orchestration framework — to run automated ION tests.

## Ansible Automation (In Progress)
At the direction of the research advisor, began developing an **Ansible**-based framework to replace the manual VM setup process — enabling an arbitrary number of ION test nodes to be spun up automatically and wired together to run multi-node DTN tests without manual configuration. The framework was well underway when the project was discontinued.

## Outcome
The project was discontinued when the lab pivoted to a different DTN implementation. Gained deep experience in cross-architecture virtualization, Linux networking, CI tooling, and infrastructure automation.

## Overview
Worked within cross-functional teams to design, build, and maintain full-stack web applications supporting university operations over three years.

## Capstone Project — Go Ohio Mobile App
Collaborated with a team of four student software engineers to extend the university's official mobile app (iOS and Android) as a senior capstone project in partnership with Ohio University IT. Designed and built a feature that automatically imports a student's class schedule and generates optimized Google Maps walking routes to the next class based on time of day — surfacing the right route at the right time without manual input.

Built using the **Modo Labs** mobile platform, **Google Maps API** for routing, and integrated with university scheduling systems to pull live course data.

## General Development Work
- Modernized and refactored legacy applications into current languages and platforms, improving maintainability and longevity
- Collaborated through **GitHub**, code reviews, and iterative development workflows
- Worked across the stack using **React**, **Angular**, **Node.js**, and **TypeScript**

Supported districtwide technology infrastructure across multiple school buildings, contributing to day-to-day operational uptime and long-term hardware reliability.

- Handled device deployment, diagnostics, repairs, and classroom hardware installations
- Maintained networking equipment including switches and UPS systems
- Built hands-on experience in hardware troubleshooting, IT support workflows, and cross-team communication
